questions.prompt: Why are warp-level reductions (using __shfl_sync) faster than shared-memory reductions?

choices.text: Warp shuffles operate directly on registers between threads in the same warp, without writing to or reading from any memory. A shared-memory reduction requires writes to shared memory, a __syncthreads() barrier, reads from shared memory, repeated for each tree-reduction step. Each shared-memory round-trip costs cycles and forces synchronization. Warp shuffles complete in a handful of cycles with no memory traffic and no explicit sync (the warp is already in lockstep). A typical warp reduction is 5 shuffle operations vs. 10+ shared-memory operations with barriers. For block-wide reductions, the optimal pattern is warp-level shuffles within each warp, then one shared-memory step to combine warp results — best of both.

questions.explanation: Warp shuffles are register-to-register data exchange, no memory involved. This is much faster than shared memory for small-group operations. Modern high-performance reduction code uses warp shuffles as the inner step.

questions.prompt: A kernel uses atomicAdd to a single global variable for a reduction. Performance is terrible. What's the fix?

choices.text: Hierarchical reduction. Single atomic to a global variable serializes all threads — every thread waits for every other thread, defeating parallelism entirely. The standard fix is hierarchical: each warp does a 5-step warp-level reduction in registers using __shfl_sync, producing one value per warp; warps in a block combine via shared memory (one __syncthreads); blocks combine via atomics or a second kernel launch. With 256 threads per block and the right structure, you go from 256 serialized atomics down to 1 atomic per block — and many blocks can issue their atomics concurrently. Speedup can be 50-100x. Atomic operations are sometimes unavoidable but should be the last step of a hierarchical pattern, never the inner loop.

questions.explanation: Atomics serialize. Hierarchical reductions push the parallelism through warp/block levels before any contention. This is the canonical fast-reduction pattern; libraries like CUB implement it under the hood.
